name: Following the Ebro River — circular from Briones
name_original: Siguiendo el Río Ebro — circular desde Briones
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Briones are characterized by expansive rolling hills and varied terrain, offering a dynamic environment for cyclists. The region features California oak woodlands, chaparral shrubs, and open areas, with elevations ranging from 400 feet up to 1,400 feet. Many routes utilize old fire roads and gravel sections, providing diverse challenges and scenic beauty. This landscape supports a network of routes suitable for different fitness levels. Best road cycling routes around Briones The most popular road cycling route is Following the Ebro River — circular from Briones, a 16.9 miles (27.2 km) trail that takes 1 hour 16 minutes to complete. This easy route offers views along the river. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Puerto de Herrera – Peñacerrada Pass loop from Briones, a difficult 29.4 miles (47.3 km) path. This route features significant elevation gains and challenging climbs. Local road cyclists also love the Peñacerrada Pass – Labastida loop from Briones, a 36.0 miles (58.0 km) trail leading through varied terrain, often completed in about 2 hours 50 minutes. Road cycling around Briones is defined by rolling hills, oak woodlands, and gravel roads. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy loops to challenging climbs. The routes in Briones are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from more than 10 reviews. More than 300 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Briones's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when road cycling in Briones?
A: Briones is characterized by expansive rolling hills and varied terrain, with elevations ranging from 400 feet up to 1,400 feet. You'll encounter California oak woodlands, chaparral shrubs, and open areas. Many routes utilize old fire roads and gravel sections, offering a dynamic and challenging environment.

Q: How many road cycling routes are available in Briones?
A: There are over 10 road cycling routes around Briones, catering to various skill levels. These include easy, moderate, and difficult options, ensuring there's a suitable ride for almost everyone.

Q: Are there any easy road cycling routes for beginners or families in Briones?
A: Yes, Briones offers easy road cycling routes suitable for beginners or families. For instance, &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e989972656/following-the-ebro-river-circular-from-briones" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Following the Ebro River — circular from Briones&lt;/a&gt; is an easy 16.9-mile (27.2 km) route that provides pleasant views along the river.

Q: What are some challenging road cycling routes in Briones for experienced riders?
A: For experienced riders seeking a challenge, Briones offers several difficult routes with significant elevation gains. A popular choice is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31757632" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de Herrera – Peñacerrada Pass loop from Briones&lt;/a&gt;, a 29.4-mile (47.3 km) path featuring demanding climbs. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/39545280" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peñacerrada Pass – Puerto de Herrera loop from Briones&lt;/a&gt;, which is even longer at 60 km and includes over 1100 meters of elevation gain.

Q: What are the best times of year to go road cycling in Briones?
A: The spring and fall generally offer the most pleasant conditions for road cycling in Briones. Spring brings vibrant wildflower displays. However, be aware that trails can become very muddy and potentially impassable for up to two weeks after significant rain, especially due to cattle activity. Gravel has been added to some popular trails to help mitigate this.

Q: Are there any notable viewpoints or landmarks along the road cycling routes?
A: Yes, Briones offers breathtaking panoramic views. From Briones Peak, the highest point, you can see Mount Diablo, the Diablo Valley, the Sacramento River, and the San Francisco Bay Area. Along your rides, you might also encounter historical sites like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5135658" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Poyo de Roldán Stone Hut&lt;/a&gt; or the scenic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3692683" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puente de Cihuri&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Can I find any water features or waterfalls near the road cycling routes?
A: While the park includes Sindicich and Maricich Lagoons, which are vital habitats, you can find impressive waterfalls in the broader region. Consider exploring highlights like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5474808" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ollerías Waterfall&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6864843" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Inglares Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;, which are part of the 'Waterfalls around Briones' guide.

Q: What do other road cyclists say about their experience in Briones?
A: The road cycling routes in Briones are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars from more than 10 reviews. Over 300 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Briones's varied terrain, often praising the challenging climbs, scenic views, and the feeling of being deep in the countryside.

Q: Are there any loop routes available for road cycling in Briones?
A: Yes, Briones is well-suited for loop routes. Many of the listed tours are circular, allowing you to start and end at the same point. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24573852" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View to castillo de Davalillo – La Vermutería loop from Briones&lt;/a&gt; offers a moderate 61 km ride.

Q: What kind of wildlife might I see while cycling in Briones?
A: Briones is home to diverse wildlife. You might spot black-tailed deer, coyotes, squirrels, red-tailed hawks, and turkey vultures. The park's grassy meadows and wooded areas provide habitats for various species, and in spring, you'll see vibrant displays of wildflowers.

Q: Are there any towns or villages worth visiting near the cycling routes?
A: Yes, the region around Briones features charming towns and villages. For instance, the historic town of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5184637" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Haro&lt;/a&gt; is a notable highlight, as is the quaint &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5474818" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Berganzo Village&lt;/a&gt;, both offering a glimpse into the local culture and history.
tips:
Documented for the first time in 1367, when Enrique de Trastámara gave it to Carlos El Malo of Navarra, it is likely that it was built during the reign of Alfonso VIII to strengthen the area's defense against Navarre.

It is Romanesque in style and is made up of a walled outer enclosure with a polygonal plan with seven sides. The keep, with four floors, has a square floor plan and is attached to the wall at its eastern end.

The castle is located on a hill next to a meander of the Ebro river, a strategic place next to the Briones castle to protect the area from Navarrese attacks.

Thanks to its location you can enjoy both the views of the town and surroundings as well as the parish itself. It is located on an elevation of the land with respect to the town of San Vicente de la Sonsierra, and where it can be seen from many angles around.

An excellent place to enjoy the excellent views of the La Rioja vineyard, surrounded by a meander of the Ebro River and with the Sierra de Cantabria in the background and the group of towns on the other bank of the river.

waypoints.name: Church of Santa María la Mayor (San Vicente de la Sonsierra)
